title: Unauthenticated cross-tenant disclosure of users and tenants via GET /api/auth/check-tenants
category: Broken Access Control / Sensitive Data Exposure
description: The check-tenants route handler performs no authentication or authorization and is not covered by the middleware matcher, so any anonymous caller can enumerate the 10 most recent tenants (company name, CR number, schema name, plan tier, regulatory context) and the 10 most recent users (email, name, role, tenantId) across the ENTIRE registry - a cross-tenant PII and infrastructure-metadata leak.
expectedBehavior: A registry-wide listing of tenants and users must require an authenticated platform-operator (the same PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist gate used by authRouter.tenantsList), and must never be reachable anonymously.
actualBehavior: GET /api/auth/check-tenants returns tenant + user records with no session check. The route is absent from middleware.ts `config.matcher`, so even the middleware never runs for it.

evidence.snippet:
users: recentUsers.map((u: any) => ({
        id: u.id,
        email: u.email,
        name: u.name,
        role: u.role,
        tenantId: u.tenantId,

matcher: [
    "/api/auth/callback/credentials",
    "/api/auth/signup",
    "/api/auth/request-reset",
    "/api/auth/reset",

reproduction: curl https://<host>/api/auth/check-tenants -> returns JSON with cross-tenant tenants[] (companyName, crNumber, schemaName) and users[] (email, name, role, tenantId), no auth required.
technicalRisk: Leaks internal Postgres schema names (aids schema-per-tenant targeting) and the full user email/role map, enabling targeted phishing, credential-stuffing and tenant reconnaissance.
businessRisk: PDPL/GDPR-class personal-data breach: customer employee emails and names exposed publicly. Reputational and regulatory exposure for a Saudi HR SaaS.
recommendation: Delete the route or gate it behind the platform-operator allowlist (reuse the PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS check from trpc/routers/auth.ts tenantsList) and add it to the middleware matcher. Return 404 to anonymous callers.
workaround: Block /api/auth/check-tenants at the edge/CDN until fixed.

title: Orphan POST /api/auth/login is an unthrottled password-verification oracle (no rate limit, no lockout, no MFA)
description: A second, custom credentials endpoint exists alongside the NextAuth flow. It validates the password with bcrypt but is NOT in the middleware matcher (so the 5/min login rate limit never applies), does NOT consult or increment the durable account-lockout state, and does NOT enforce MFA. It therefore permits unlimited online password guessing against real accounts and, on success, discloses the account role and tenantId. The frontend uses signIn()/callback/credentials instead, so this route is unreferenced dead code that still serves live traffic.
expectedBehavior: All credential-checking endpoints must share the same brute-force controls (IP rate limit + durable per-account lockout + MFA gate) as the NextAuth authorize() path, or not exist.
actualBehavior: The route calls bcrypt compare and returns 200/401 with no throttling, no lockout accounting, and no MFA. It is missing from middleware.ts matcher entirely.

evidence.snippet:
const isLoginAttempt = pathname.startsWith("/api/auth/callback/credentials");
  if (isLoginAttempt && !checkRateLimit(`login:${ip}`, 5, 60_000)) {

reproduction: Loop POST /api/auth/login with {email, password} candidates - no 429 and no account lockout is ever triggered; a 200 confirms a valid credential pair and reveals role/tenantId.
technicalRisk: Bypasses every brute-force mitigation the team built for the NextAuth path; enables password spraying and credential stuffing at full speed.
businessRisk: Account takeover of HR/payroll admins, leading to PII and payroll fraud.
recommendation: Delete this route (the app authenticates via NextAuth). If a JSON login API is genuinely needed, route it through the same lockout + rate-limit + MFA logic and add it to the middleware matcher.
workaround: Add /api/auth/login to the middleware matcher with the login rate limit, or block it at the edge.

title: No absolute session lifetime and stale role/privileges in JWT (no revocation on role change or deactivation)
exploitability: moderate - requires prior compromise or a privilege change window
description: The JWT session uses a 30-minute rolling idle window (maxAge 30m, updateAge 5m) with NO absolute cap, so an active session can be extended indefinitely. The jwt callback only re-hydrates employeeId on later requests - it never re-reads the user's role or verifies the account still exists/is enabled. Consequently a role downgrade, a role removal, or an account deletion is not reflected until the idle window lapses (up to 30 min), and there is no server-side revocation because the strategy is stateless JWT. The users table has no 'disabled/status' column, so an operator cannot force-logout or lock out an active token at all.
expectedBehavior: Role/authorization changes and account deactivation should take effect on the next request (re-read role from DB in the jwt callback or check a token version / session revocation list), and sessions should have an absolute maximum lifetime.
actualBehavior: Role is copied only at first sign-in; later requests keep the old role. No absolute expiry, no revocation, no account-disabled concept.

evidence.snippet: session: { strategy: 'jwt', maxAge: 30 * 60, updateAge: 5 * 60 },

reproduction: Sign in as a user, have an admin change that user's role to a lower one; the active session keeps admin capabilities until the 30-min idle window expires. Deleting the user does not invalidate the live token.
technicalRisk: Privilege changes and deprovisioning are not enforced in real time; a stolen JWT cannot be revoked.
businessRisk: Terminated employees retain access for the idle window; least-privilege violations during role transitions.
recommendation: Re-read role (and an is_active/token_version) from the DB in the jwt callback on each request, add an absolute session cap, and add an account-status column so operators can force revocation.
workaround: Reduce maxAge; instruct admins that role changes take effect after the idle window.

title: Account lockout silently disabled by schema drift - lockout columns absent from Drizzle schema
description: The durable account-lockout code reads/writes failed_login_attempts and locked_until via raw SQL wrapped in try/catch that swallows all errors and 'disables lockout until migrated' when the columns are missing. Those columns are defined ONLY in migration 0008; they are NOT present in the Drizzle schema (users.ts). Because CLAUDE.md documents `pnpm db:push` (schema-push) as a workflow, a push from the drifted schema can DROP failed_login_attempts/locked_until, silently turning brute-force lockout off with no error surfaced. Prior memory notes already flag attendance schema drift in this repo.
expectedBehavior: Lockout columns should be part of the source-of-truth Drizzle schema so db:push preserves them, and a missing-column condition should be logged loudly, not swallowed.
actualBehavior: Columns live only in a hand-written migration; the schema object omits them; failures are swallowed and lockout becomes a no-op.

evidence.snippet:
mfaSecret: text("mfa_secret"),
  employeeId: uuid("employee_id"),
  createdAt: timestamp("created_at").defaultNow().notNull(),

ALTER TABLE public.users ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS failed_login_attempts INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
ALTER TABLE public.users ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS locked_until TIMESTAMP;

reproduction: Run pnpm db:push from the current schema (which lacks the columns) against a DB that has them -> Drizzle proposes dropping failed_login_attempts/locked_until; after that, recordFailedAttempt/getLockState throw and are swallowed, so accounts never lock.
technicalRisk: Silent loss of the primary brute-force defense on the NextAuth login path.
businessRisk: Undetected regression enabling password guessing at scale.
recommendation: Add failedLoginAttempts and lockedUntil to the Drizzle users schema so they are managed and preserved; replace the silent catch with a one-time warning log/metric.
workaround: Use db:migrate (not db:push) in all environments; verify columns exist post-deploy.

title: Password reset token written to server logs unconditionally; email delivery not wired
description: request-reset builds the full reset URL containing the raw (un-hashed) token and logs it via console.info in ALL environments, including production - there is no NODE_ENV guard. Email delivery is a TODO, so in production the log is the ONLY place the token exists, and anyone with log/observability access (or a log sink integration) can complete an account takeover. Storing only the SHA-256 hash in the DB is good, but logging the plaintext token defeats it.
expectedBehavior: The raw token must only ever leave the server inside the emailed link; it must never be written to logs. In non-prod, gate any debug logging behind an explicit flag.
actualBehavior: console.info logs email + full reset URL (raw token) on every request-reset for an existing user, unconditionally.
reproduction: POST /api/auth/request-reset with a registered email; inspect server logs -> the full reset URL with a usable raw token appears.
technicalRisk: Account takeover for anyone able to read application logs.
businessRisk: Privileged-account compromise; PDPL breach if logs are shipped to third-party sinks.
recommendation: Remove the token log line; wire delivery through @hrms-app/email. If a dev fallback is needed, gate it behind NODE_ENV !== 'production' AND an explicit DEV flag, and log only that a link was generated (no token).
workaround: Redact reset URLs at the log pipeline; treat existing logs as compromised and expire outstanding tokens.

title: MFA is opt-in only and not enforced by any policy (privileged roles can operate without MFA)
description: TOTP MFA is only enforced when the individual user has already enrolled a secret (if (user.mfaSecret)). There is no organization policy requiring MFA for high-privilege roles (super_admin, hr_manager, payroll_admin), and nothing in signup/onboarding drives enrollment. A compromised password alone is sufficient for full admin access on any account that has not voluntarily enrolled.
expectedBehavior: Privileged roles should be required to enrol MFA (block or step-up until enrolled), configurable per tenant.
actualBehavior: MFA gate is skipped entirely for any account without mfaSecret; no role-based enforcement exists.
reproduction: Create/sign in as an hr_manager without enrolling MFA; login succeeds with password only.
technicalRisk: Password-only compromise yields full admin access to HR/payroll data.
businessRisk: Elevated ATO risk for the highest-value accounts.
recommendation: Add a tenant policy that requires MFA for privileged roles; block access (or force enrollment) until a secret is set.
workaround: Operationally require admins to enrol MFA.

title: In-memory middleware rate limiting is per-instance and ineffective on serverless/multi-instance
description: Rate limiting is backed by a module-level in-memory Map. On Vercel/serverless (and any horizontally-scaled deploy) each instance has its own map and the map resets on cold start, so the 5/min login limit and other limits are enforced only per-instance - an attacker rotating across instances or after cold starts largely evades them. The durable per-account lockout mitigates this for the NextAuth path, but the standalone /api/auth/login route (AUTH-002) has neither. Redis (Upstash) is already configured in env but not used for rate limiting.
expectedBehavior: Rate limiting for auth endpoints should use a shared store (Redis/Upstash) so limits are global across instances.
actualBehavior: Limits use a per-process Map that resets on cold start.
reproduction: Send login attempts spread across cold starts / instances; aggregate throughput exceeds the intended 5/min.
businessRisk: Higher ATO risk under distributed attack.
recommendation: Move auth rate limiting to Upstash Redis (already in env) keyed by IP and by account; keep the durable DB lockout as defense-in-depth.
workaround: Add a WAF/edge rate limit in front of /api/auth/*.

title: MFA lacks recovery/backup codes and per-account TOTP attempt throttling
description: MFA enrollment stores only a TOTP secret; there are no backup/recovery codes, so a user who loses their authenticator is locked out and requires manual DB intervention (clearing mfa_secret). Additionally, a wrong TOTP at login deliberately does not count toward account lockout (the password was correct), and the only throttle on TOTP guessing is the per-instance IP rate limit (see AUTH-007). With a +/-1 step window (3 valid codes per 1,000,000) online guessing is impractical but not rate-limited at the account level.
expectedBehavior: Provide single-use hashed backup codes at enrollment and throttle repeated failed TOTP attempts per account.
actualBehavior: No backup codes; TOTP failures are unbounded at the account level.

evidence.snippet:
// MFA gate (C4) ... A wrong/missing code
        // does not count toward lockout (the password was correct).

reproduction: Enrol MFA; there is no recovery-code output. Submit repeated wrong TOTP codes with a correct password - no account-level lockout accrues.
technicalRisk: TOTP guessing has only a weak IP throttle; account recovery requires manual DB edits.
businessRisk: Support burden and lockouts; slightly weakened MFA.
recommendation: Generate 8-10 single-use backup codes (store hashed) at enrollment; add a per-account failed-TOTP counter that triggers a temporary MFA lock.
workaround: Document an admin runbook to reset mfa_secret on request after identity verification.

title: Self-serve signup mints a tenant super_admin with no email verification or CR validation
description: Signup creates a tenant and inserts a user with role 'super_admin' and no email verification (emailVerified is never set) and no CR-number validation. Anyone can register a tenant bound to an email they do not control. Blast radius is limited because super_admin is tenant-scoped and cross-tenant registry access is separately gated by the PLATFORM_ADMIN_EMAILS allowlist (good), so the new super_admin only controls its own freshly created (empty) tenant - but it does enable spam-tenant creation and unverified-email accounts. There is also no meaningful anti-automation on /api/auth/signup (only the loose 10/sec auth-route limit).
expectedBehavior: Require email verification before activating the account, validate the CR number format, and rate-limit signups.
actualBehavior: super_admin created immediately, unverified email, no CR validation.
reproduction: POST /api/auth/signup with any email -> a tenant + super_admin account is created with emailVerified null.
technicalRisk: Unverified privileged accounts; tenant/DB-schema spam (each signup creates a new schema).
businessRisk: Resource abuse and unverified-identity accounts in a compliance-sensitive product.
recommendation: Add email verification before first login, validate CR number, and rate-limit signup (shared store).
workaround: Monitor tenant creation; disable open signup if not required.

title: Reset token transmitted in URL query string (history / Referer / access-log leakage)
category: Password Recovery / Sensitive Data in URL
description: The reset link carries the raw token (and email) as URL query parameters. Tokens in URLs are recorded in browser history, may leak via the Referer header to any third-party asset on the reset page, and land in server/proxy access logs. Token TTL (1h), single-use and hashed storage limit the window, but query-string delivery is discouraged.
expectedBehavior: Prefer delivering the token via a path segment with a strict no-referrer policy, or accept the token in a POST body / fragment; ensure Referrer-Policy: no-referrer on the reset page.
actualBehavior: email + raw token are in the query string and read client-side from useSearchParams.

evidence.snippet: const resetUrl = `${base}/reset-password?email=${encodeURIComponent(email)}&token=${rawToken}`;

const email = params.get("email") ?? "";
  const token = params.get("token") ?? "";

reproduction: Open a reset link; the token is visible in the address bar and browser history and is sent as Referer to any external resource loaded by the page.
technicalRisk: Token leakage via history/Referer/logs within the 1h window.
businessRisk: Marginal ATO risk if a reset URL leaks.
recommendation: Set Referrer-Policy: no-referrer on the reset route and keep the reset page free of third-party assets; consider a path-based token.
workaround: Short TTL (already 1h) and single-use (already enforced) limit exposure.

title: Diagnostics endpoint discloses env wiring (AUTH_SECRET length, DB host/port) outside production
description: The debug endpoint returns AUTH_SECRET length, AUTH_URL/NEXTAUTH_URL values, DB hostname and port, NODE_ENV and demo-mode state to anonymous callers. It is correctly disabled in production unless ENABLE_DEBUG_ENDPOINT=true (good), but in any non-production/preview environment it is fully open and leaks internal hostnames and the secret length. Preview deployments of a SaaS are frequently internet-reachable.
expectedBehavior: Diagnostics should require authentication (platform-operator) even in non-prod, and should never echo secret metadata such as AUTH_SECRET length.
actualBehavior: Anonymous GET returns env wiring in all non-prod environments.

evidence.snippet:
hasAuthSecret: !!authSecret,
    authSecretLength: authSecret?.length ?? 0,
    ...
    hasDbUrl: !!dbUrl,
    dbHost,
    dbPort,

reproduction: GET /api/auth/debug on a preview/staging deployment -> returns dbHost, dbPort, authSecretLength, etc.
technicalRisk: Recon aid (internal hostnames, secret length) on non-prod environments.
recommendation: Require platform-operator auth for this endpoint in all environments and drop authSecretLength/dbHost/dbPort from the payload.

title: Password minimum length of 8 and no breached-password / max-length-independent checks
description: The shared password policy requires 8+ chars with upper/lower/digit/special (good composition, and a 128 max that safely bounds bcrypt input). For a product holding payroll/PII, an 8-char minimum is on the low side; there is no check against known-breached passwords (e.g. HaveIBeenPwned k-anonymity) and no MFA-required compensating control for admins (see AUTH-006). Note bcrypt truncates input at 72 bytes - acceptable here given the 128-char cap and complexity, but worth documenting.
expectedBehavior: Raise the minimum to 12 for privileged roles and/or add a breached-password screen; document the bcrypt 72-byte behavior.

evidence.snippet:
export const passwordSchema = z
  .string()
  .min(8, "Password must be at least 8 characters")
  .max(128, "Password is too long")
  .regex(/[A-Z]/, ...)

reproduction: Register with an 8-char password like 'Passw0r!' - accepted.
technicalRisk: Slightly weaker credentials for admin accounts.
recommendation: Increase minimum length (12 for privileged roles), add a breached-password check, and pair with enforced MFA for admins.
